hero.title: Scaling First-Party Commerce Across D2C Brands
hero.subtitle: Built a shared commerce platform that launched three brand storefronts in 8 weeks, reduced marketplace dependency, and established the first cross-brand design system for the Honasa Consumer portfolio.

points: Honasa Consumer operated 3 D2C brands—Mamaearth, The Derma Co., and Aqualogica—each generating most revenue through third-party marketplaces with little owned-channel presence.
Margin pressure from marketplace commissions and limited customer data ownership were creating compounding business risk as the brand portfolio grew.
No existing UX process, design system, or shared component library existed across the brand portfolio. Each team operated independently.
A hard 8-week deadline tied to a seasonal sale window meant there was no room for process setup followed by design—both had to happen in parallel.

problem.statement: How might we build a scalable, first-party commerce experience across three distinct brands—without duplicating engineering or design effort—that could ship in 8 weeks and be owned by a lean team?

points: No prior UX process or pattern library to build on
Three brands with distinct visual identities and customer expectations
Hard 8-week launch deadline tied to a seasonal sale window
Small design team (2 people) supporting 4 engineers in parallel
Speed vs. long-term system quality tradeoff at every decision point

ownership: End-to-end UX design for all three brand storefronts across PDP, cart, checkout, and post-purchase
Defining the shared component architecture and brand token layer
Collaborating with engineering lead on component boundaries and API contracts
Establishing the design-to-dev handoff process from scratch under time pressure
collaboration: Product Manager — feature prioritization and scope management under deadline
Engineering Lead — component feasibility, shared backend boundaries
Brand Teams — non-negotiable visual identity constraints per brand
QA — acceptance criteria for UX edge cases and responsive behavior

inputs: Marketplace analytics to identify high-drop-off purchase stages across existing channels
Competitor D2C audits across beauty and personal care verticals to understand table-stakes patterns
User interviews with existing Mamaearth customers about friction in purchase flows
Brand team sessions to surface visual identity constraints and non-negotiable design elements
insights: 68% of drop-offs happened at the Product Detail Page and cart stages—not checkout as originally assumed
Users trusted Mamaearth's ingredient transparency deeply; they expected the same signal from sibling brands
All three brands shared identical commerce logic; they diverged only in visual language and tone
Engineering had standardized on a single backend. The duplication was entirely in the frontend—and the design layer
implications: Design energy should focus on PDP and cart—not checkout optimization, which was already well-understood
Ingredient and trust signals needed to be first-class components, not brand-specific afterthoughts
A single component library with brand-level theming was achievable and preferable to three separate codebases
Keeping component logic separate from brand presentation would be the key architectural decision

decisions.title: Shared Commerce Backbone with Brand Token Overrides
decisions.problem: Building separate commerce experiences per brand would mean 3x the design and dev effort. Any future improvement would need to be replicated three times, creating compounding maintenance cost.
decisions.decision: Designed a single component library where all commerce logic—PDP, cart, checkout, post-purchase—lives in shared components. Brand identity is applied through a token layer covering colors, typography, and imagery only.
decisions.tradeoff: Reduced flexibility in early stages. Brand teams couldn't request bespoke layouts. This created friction initially but was essential for long-term maintainability and speed.
decisions.impact: All three storefronts shipped in 8 weeks with one designer and two engineers per brand sprint. Changes to core flows now propagate to all brands simultaneously.

decisions.title: MVP Scoping: Revenue-Critical Flows Only
decisions.problem: Stakeholders wanted wishlists, product recommendations, loyalty programs, and bundle offers in v1. Delivering all of this would push the launch past the sale window.
decisions.decision: Mapped every requested feature against its estimated revenue contribution. Kept only PDP, cart, checkout, and order confirmation. Documented the rationale for every deferral explicitly.
decisions.tradeoff: Delayed personalization and discovery features by one quarter, which frustrated some stakeholders initially. But core flows shipped on time with high quality and no post-launch critical bugs.
decisions.impact: On-time launch across all three brands. The deferred feature list became the v2 roadmap, which was funded directly off the back of v1 results.

decisions.title: Trust Signals as System-Level Components
decisions.problem: Research showed ingredient transparency was a primary purchase driver for Mamaearth customers. Other brands hadn't thought about this systematically, risking inconsistent trust signals across the portfolio.
decisions.decision: Built ingredient highlights, trust badges, and certification displays as reusable PDP components available to all brands—not hardcoded per brand as one-offs.
decisions.tradeoff: Required more upfront component design time and engineering spec work. But avoided brand-specific components that would be impossible to audit or improve across the portfolio.
decisions.impact: All three brands adopted trust components in v1. Mamaearth saw measurable improvement in PDP-to-cart conversion. Other brands adopted the pattern in subsequent releases independently.

shared: Product Detail Page (PDP) layout, interaction patterns, and sticky CTA behavior
Cart state management, line-item components, and quantity controls
Checkout flow: address entry, payment selection, order confirmation
Responsive layout grid and spacing token scale
Navigation shell, search patterns, and filter components
brandSpecific: Color tokens: primary, accent, surface, and background per brand
Typography: font families, scale, and weight assignments
Hero imagery and campaign overlay visual language
Product card art direction and photography style

highlights: Single-page checkout reduced form steps from 6 to 3 across all brands, with address autocomplete reducing input friction
Ingredient transparency panel surfaced as a persistent, keyboard-accessible side-drawer on the PDP—not a modal
Cart design unified product thumbnails, quantity controls, and cross-sell slots into a single cohesive component that could be themed per brand
Post-purchase state included order tracking entry point and a contextual cross-sell recommendation—not just a confirmation message

points: Launched all three brand storefronts within the 8-week window, meeting the seasonal sale deadline
Enabled owned-channel commerce at scale, reducing dependency on third-party marketplace commission structures
Established the first cross-brand design system in the Honasa Consumer portfolio—a foundation that persisted through two more brand additions
New brand (Aqualogica Glow) was onboarded onto the system in 3 weeks—down from 8 weeks for the initial three
Two additional designers onboarded in Q2 with no ramp-up from scratch; the system documentation became onboarding material

tradeoffs: Personalization and recommendation features were deferred to v2. The right call given the deadline, but it meant a less differentiated initial experience than the brand teams had envisioned.

The brand token system was designed quickly under deadline pressure and required significant refactoring 3 months later as more brands were added. Investing more time in token architecture upfront would have paid back quickly.
User research was compressed to 5 days of interviews. More discovery time would have surfaced mobile-specific interaction patterns that had to be addressed in a post-launch patch.
Engineering handoff relied heavily on verbal communication and annotated Figma frames—no Storybook or component documentation. Setting this up earlier would have reduced back-and-forth significantly.

timeline.label: Discovery
timeline.detail: Stakeholder interviews, competitor audits, analytics review, and constraint mapping across all three brands.

timeline.label: System Architecture
timeline.detail: Defined shared component boundaries, brand token structure, and MVP feature scope through a prioritization matrix.

timeline.label: Design & Prototype
timeline.detail: Designed all core flows across three brands simultaneously using the shared system. Internal reviews with brand teams and engineering.

timeline.label: Engineering Handoff
timeline.detail: Component-by-component spec delivery with edge case documentation, responsive specs, and interaction notes.

timeline.label: Launch
timeline.detail: Staged release across Mamaearth, The Derma Co., and Aqualogica storefronts. Real-time monitoring and rapid iteration.

timeline.label: System Iteration
timeline.detail: Token architecture refactor, Storybook setup, and v2 roadmap scoped from live user behavior data and brand team feedback.

takeaway: Scalable product systems aren't built by adding features—they're built by ruthlessly separating what varies from what doesn't, and making that separation explicit at the very start.
